questions.prompt: For distortionless transmission through a filter, the phase response must be:
questions.explanation: Linear phase ∠H(ω) = −ωt_d means every frequency component gets the same time delay t_d. The output is y(t) = x(t − t_d) — a delayed copy with no shape change. Constant phase causes different delays for different frequencies.

questions.prompt: If ∠H(ω) = −3ω over the passband, the time delay of the output is:
questions.explanation: Comparing ∠H(ω) = −ωt_d with ∠H(ω) = −3ω gives t_d = 3 seconds. The output is y(t) = x(t − 3), delayed by 3 seconds.

questions.prompt: A filter passes two frequencies ω₁ = 10 and ω₂ = 20 with ∠H(ω) = C (constant). The time delays experienced are:
questions.explanation: Phase shift of C at frequency ω corresponds to time delay C/ω. So ω₁ = 10 gets delay C/10, ω₂ = 20 gets delay C/20. Since these differ, the signal shape is distorted.

questions.prompt: The complete ideal LPF response is H(ω) = P_{2B}(ω)·e^{−jωt_d}. What does P_{2B}(ω) represent?
options: A rectangular pulse of width 2B centred at ω = 0
questions.correctAnswer: A rectangular pulse of width 2B centred at ω = 0
questions.explanation: P_{2B}(ω) is the rectangular (rect/gate) function: equals 1 for |ω| ≤ B and 0 for |ω| > B. It defines the ideal passband. The total width is 2B (from −B to +B).

Flat magnitude prevents amplitude distortion (all frequencies pass equally). Linear phase prevents phase distortion (all frequencies delayed equally). Both conditions must hold over the passband for truly distortionless output.

questions.prompt: If two frequency components have the same time delay through a filter, there is:
questions.explanation: Same time delay = same phase behaviour = no relative phase shift between components. Combined with equal magnitude gain, the output shape matches the input shape, just delayed.

questions.prompt: A constant phase ∠H(ω) = π/4 means the equivalent time delay at ω = 100 rad/s is:
questions.explanation: Time delay = phase/ω = (π/4)/100 = π/400 seconds. At a different frequency the delay would differ — that's why constant phase causes distortion.

questions.prompt: Which of these phase functions is 'linear' in the distortionless sense?
questions.explanation: Distortionless requires ∠H(ω) = −ωt_d — directly proportional to ω with no additive constant. ∠H = −5ω gives t_d = 5 s. The option −5ω + π/3 includes a constant offset which adds a fixed phase shift but still preserves shape (generalised linear phase), though the strict definition requires passing through origin.

options: Some frequencies are amplified more than others
Some frequencies experience different time delays
The filter has no phase response
The magnitude response is not rectangular
questions.correctAnswer: Some frequencies experience different time delays
questions.explanation: Phase distortion occurs when different frequency components experience different time delays, so they recombine at the output with altered relative timing. This changes the shape of the composite signal even if all magnitudes are preserved.

questions.prompt: Over the passband of an ideal LPF, the magnitude |H(ω)| and phase ∠H(ω) are:
questions.explanation: In the passband (|ω| < B), the ideal LPF has unity magnitude (no attenuation) and linear phase (pure time delay). This is H(ω) = 1·e^{−jωtd} = e^{−jωtd}.

questions.prompt: A signal x(t) = cos(t) + cos(3t) passes through a filter with ∠H(ω) = −2ω. The output is:
questions.explanation: Linear phase ∠H = −2ω → t_d = 2. Each component is delayed by t_d = 2: cos(1·(t−2)) + cos(3·(t−2)). Both shift by the same 2 seconds, so no distortion — just delay.

questions.prompt: The parameter t_d in ∠H(ω) = −ωt_d is called:
questions.explanation: t_d is the propagation delay (or time delay). It represents the uniform delay applied to all frequency components in the passband. Also related to group delay τ_g = −d∠H/dω = t_d (constant for linear phase).
